description: A person waiting at a doorway may 'whano', and so may a bottle left upright on a shelf; both hold an upright posture or position, though the word says nothing about why. 'pa whano' marks the change into that position: to stand up.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips as if for 'w' while letting unvoiced air pass through for 'wh', then open into stressed central 'a'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 'n', keep the airflow through the nose, and finish with rounded middle-height back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Breathy 'wh' opens into the broad stressed vowel, then the nasal and rounded ending draw the sound back into balance. The mouth opens fully before finding a quieter, steadier finish.
usage_notes: 'whano' names the posture or position itself. Inchoative 'pa' distinguishes standing up, while 'rihe' (rise) names upward movement more generally and need not end in a standing posture.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Standing is one of the four ordinary bodily postures used for mindfulness practice. 'whano' names the posture, not a spiritual achievement, so attention must still be stated or supplied by context.

semantic_domains.activity: holding or entering an upright posture
semantic_domains.physical: upright bodily posture or object orientation
semantic_domains.spatial: vertical position relative to a surface
